case_name: Leach   v North East Ambulance Service NHS Foundation Trust [2020] EWHC 2914 (QB) (30 October 2020)

evidence_index.text: On 18 September 2016 the claimant, at the age of 41 years, (born 15.02.75) had the misfortune to suffer a subarachnoid haemorrhage ('SAH') as a result of a ruptured aneurysm.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Breach of duty in this case is admitted insofar as it is accepted that there was a 31-minute negligent period of delay in the ambulance arriving at the claimant's house.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The evidence as to causation is essentially a matter for expert psychiatrists and, to that end, I have received evidence from Dr Smith, instructed on behalf of the claimant and Dr Bowers, instructed on behalf of the defendant.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: When the claimant suffered the SAH, she was on her own, at her home in Ingleby Barwick, Stockton-on-Tees.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: She describes feeling 'a pop and gush in [her] head and an immense pain'. She recalls losing consciousness and vomiting.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The ambulance eventually arrived at 16.11. The claimant was initially taken to University Hospital of North Tees but, thereafter, she was transferred to the James Cook University Hospital.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: I find that the period of the 31-minute delay made a material contribution to the claimant's PTSD and that an apportionment exercise is not permissible in these circumstances.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: Dr Smith maintained that it was simply not possible to pinpoint a moment when the PTSD was triggered during the time between the happening of the SAH and the arrival of the ambulance.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr Smith

evidence_index.text: Dr Bowers answered my question which was directed at establishing when he thought she was going to develop PTSD, in any event. His (wholly unexpected) response was that she was destined to suffer 'full blown' PTSD as from the moment when she suffered the SAH.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr Bowers

evidence_index.text: It is, to my mind, in the realms of speculation to attempt to identify a fixed time when the claimant had suffered sufficient trauma such that she was likely to go on to suffer PTSD.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

initial_state.chief_complaint: Severe headache and loss of consciousness
initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient experienced a 'pop and gush' in her head followed by immense pain, loss of consciousness, and vomiting. She managed to call 999 after regaining consciousness.

focused_exam.neurological: Patient was in and out of consciousness, severe headache reported

requestables.name: CT Head
requestables.description: CT scan of the head to assess for hemorrhage

reveal.result_summary: Imaging performed but findings not detailed in judgment

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient experiences severe headache and calls for emergency services

timeline_phases.name: Emergency Response
timeline_phases.description: Delay in ambulance arrival and subsequent hospital transfer

decision_points.prompt: What should be the priority in managing this patient?

options.description: Immediate transfer to hospital with neurosurgical capabilities
options.clinical_reasoning: Urgent neurosurgical intervention required for SAH

options.description: Monitor at home until ambulance arrives
options.clinical_reasoning: Delay in care could worsen outcomes

actual_action_defendant.description: Patient was left waiting for ambulance

expected_action_court.description: Immediate hospital transfer should have been prioritized
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence supports urgent care for SAH

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Delay in ambulance arrival contributed to PTSD

patient_outcome.description: Patient developed PTSD following the traumatic event
patient_outcome.long_term_effects: Fear of being alone, anxiety about ambulances

key_findings: 31-minute delay in ambulance arrival contributed to PTSD
Breach of duty admitted by defendant

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Timely ambulance arrival could have mitigated PTSD
